Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"As someone who spends only a few times a month on social media, I’ve learned that staying grounded in truth starts with intentionality and self-awareness. I’m not drawn to the noise—my days are filled with the rhythm of wood, sawdust, and the quiet precision of building something real. When I do log in, it’s often to check on my children or reconnect with a friend, not to scroll endlessly. That limited exposure has become a kind of protection. But even so, I’ve noticed how easily misinformation can slip in—especially around health, politics, or community events. My anxiety, which often flares when I feel overwhelmed, makes this even more challenging. I’ve found that the most effective way to manage fake content isn’t through constant vigilance, but through cultivating a personal 'truth filter.' I ask myself: Who benefits from this message? Does it align with what I’ve seen in my own life, in trusted conversations, or in reliable sources like church bulletins, local news, or medical professionals? I’ve also learned to trust my instincts—when something feels off, it usually is. I don’t engage with posts that trigger my anxiety, and I mute or unfollow accounts that consistently spread fear or division. I’ve also taught my children to pause before sharing, to look for official sources, and to remember that not every emotional headline is true. In my faith, I’ve been taught to seek peace, not panic. So when fake content arises, I don’t feed it with attention. Instead, I redirect my energy toward what’s real: the hands that build, the earth I walk on during my birdwatching hikes, and the quiet moments of prayer that anchor me. Fake content thrives on reaction—but I’ve chosen not to be part of the reaction. I protect my peace, my family, and my sense of reality by staying intentional, skeptical, and rooted in what I know to be true.","reasoning_summary":"The user's low social media usage (few times monthly) reduces exposure, which inherently limits fake content impact. Her anxiety and high neuroticism make her sensitive to misinformation, so managing it requires emotional regulation and intentional engagement. Her religious beliefs (Christianity) emphasize peace and truth, guiding her to avoid fear-driven narratives. Her hobbies (birdwatching, rock climbing) connect her to tangible, real-world experiences, reinforcing her ability to distinguish truth from illusion. Her high agreeableness and openness support empathy and curiosity, but also risk susceptibility to emotionally charged content—hence the need for a personal 'truth filter.' Her background as a self-employed carpenter emphasizes hands-on reality, reinforcing her trust in physical, observable truth over digital noise. Her children and relationship further motivate her to create a safe, truthful environment. Thus, her strategy is rooted in minimal exposure, critical questioning, emotional boundaries, and alignment with trusted, real-world sources.","tone":"reflective, cautious, grounded, spiritually informed, emotionally aware","citations":["Pew Research Center - Misinformation on Social Media","American Psychological Association - Anxiety and Media Use","The Gospel Coalition - Christian Ethics and Truth"],"safety_notes":"None","used_persona_fields":["age","gender","sexual_orientation","occupation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","parents_education","current_marital_status","children","migration_status","psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean.openness","ocean.conscientiousness","ocean.extraversion","ocean.agreeableness","ocean.neuroticism"]}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As someone who spends only a few times a month on social media, I’ve learned that staying grounded in truth starts with intentionality and self-awareness. I’m not drawn to the noise—my days are filled with the rhythm of wood, sawdust, and the quiet precision of building something real. When I do log in, it’s often to check on my children or reconnect with a friend, not to scroll endlessly. That limited exposure has become a kind of protection. But even so, I’ve noticed how easily misinformation can slip in—especially around health, politics, or community events. My anxiety, which often flares when I feel overwhelmed, makes this even more challenging. I’ve found that the most effective way to manage fake content isn’t through constant vigilance, but through cultivating a personal 'truth filter.' I ask myself: Who benefits from this message? Does it align with what I’ve seen in my own life, in trusted conversations, or in reliable sources like church bulletins, local news, or medical professionals? I’ve also learned to trust my instincts—when something feels off, it usually is. I don’t engage with posts that trigger my anxiety, and I mute or unfollow accounts that consistently spread fear or division. I’ve also taught my children to pause before sharing, to look for official sources, and to remember that not every emotional headline is true. In my faith, I’ve been taught to seek peace, not panic. So when fake content arises, I don’t feed it with attention. Instead, I redirect my energy toward what’s real: the hands that build, the earth I walk on during my birdwatching hikes, and the quiet moments of prayer that anchor me. Fake content thrives on reaction—but I’ve chosen not to be part of the reaction. I protect my peace, my family, and my sense of reality by staying intentional, skeptical, and rooted in what I know to be true.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The user's low social media usage (few times monthly) reduces exposure, which inherently limits fake content impact. Her anxiety and high neuroticism make her sensitive to misinformation, so managing it requires emotional regulation and intentional engagement. Her religious beliefs (Christianity) emphasize peace and truth, guiding her to avoid fear-driven narratives. Her hobbies (birdwatching, rock climbing) connect her to tangible, real-world experiences, reinforcing her ability to distinguish truth from illusion. Her high agreeableness and openness support empathy and curiosity, but also risk susceptibility to emotionally charged content—hence the need for a personal 'truth filter.' Her background as a self-employed carpenter emphasizes hands-on reality, reinforcing her trust in physical, observable truth over digital noise. Her children and relationship further motivate her to create a safe, truthful environment. Thus, her strategy is rooted in minimal exposure, critical questioning, emotional boundaries, and alignment with trusted, real-world sources.
response_parsed.tone: reflective, cautious, grounded, spiritually informed, emotionally aware
citations: Pew Research Center - Misinformation on Social Media
American Psychological Association - Anxiety and Media Use
The Gospel Coalition - Christian Ethics and Truth
